questionText: Which method correctly decides whether a golden ticket result was close to theory in this sweet game? A fair jar has 12 sweets: 3 golden, 4 pink, and 5 blue. One sweet is picked and put back each time. After 36 picks, golden was picked 11 times.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can compare theoretical and experimental probability
issues: [difficulty_mismatch] | The item is mathematically correct and clear, but its labeled difficulty is too high. | This is not a hard item: students only need to identify the correct comparison between experimental probability 11/36 and theoretical probability 3/12. | The cognitive demand is closer to medium because it involves a contextual probability comparison method, but not multi-step reasoning, error analysis, reverse-working, or evaluating a substantial claim. | Fix: Relabel the item as medium difficulty. | Fix: Or increase complexity to hard by asking students to judge whether the result is close to theory and justify using equivalent fractions, expected counts, or a claim to evaluate. | Fix: If keeping it hard, strengthen distractors so they reflect richer misconceptions about comparing expected and observed results, not just obvious number-matching errors.
